title: Making a candy board for winter bees
subtitle: Emergency feed that sits above the cluster without disturbing it.
excerpt: Fondant from a garden centre works, but a home-made candy board gives you exact control over quantity and sits flatter above the cluster. Made from icing sugar and a little water, it hardens into a firm slab that bees eat from below as stores run low.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit, nationalbeeunit.com. BBKA winter preparation guidance. Pearson, F., Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: A slab of hard-set sugar paste placed directly above the winter cluster to provide emergency stores without opening the hive for feeding.
The tight ball of bees that forms in the hive from late October, vibrating to generate heat and moving slowly upward through their stores as winter progresses.

content.text: A
candy board
is the simplest winter supplement you can give a light hive. The
winter cluster

rises through its stores as winter progresses; by February a colony that started with adequate honey may have consumed much of what was directly above the cluster. A candy board sat on top of the frames gives them something to reach before they starve.
What you need
Icing sugar: 2 kg per board.
Water: 120 to 150 ml.
A baking tray or shallow wooden frame cut to the internal dimensions of your hive, typically 460 mm by 460 mm for a National hive.
Baking paper to line the tray.
Making the candy

Sift the icing sugar into a bowl to remove lumps. Add water a tablespoon at a time, mixing between additions. The target consistency is a stiff paste, it should hold its shape when pressed but not be crumbly dry. Too wet and it stays sticky; too dry and it crumbles when bees try to feed from it.

Press the paste into the lined tray to a depth of about 25 mm. Smooth the top flat. Leave to set in a cool dry room overnight. The board will harden as it dries and can be lifted cleanly off the baking paper.

Do not add pollen substitute or supplements to winter candy. These can trigger the colony to start brood rearing in January when there is no natural pollen coming in, which stresses the cluster and can accelerate stores depletion. Plain icing sugar only.
Placing the board

Place the candy board directly onto the top bars, candy side down so the cluster can reach it from below. Replace the crown board on top with its feed hole open to allow moisture to escape. Put the roof on and leave the colony undisturbed.

The candy can be placed any time from October onward. It does not replace adequate autumn feeding; a colony that went into winter with inadequate honey stores needs the same again as supplemental sugar syrup in September, not a candy board in November. The board is a top-up, not a primary winter store.
Checking and replacing

You can check the candy board without opening the hive. On a mild January day above 10°C, briefly lift the roof and crown board to see how much candy remains. If the board is more than half consumed, prepare a replacement for the next warm spell.

Alternatively, heft the hive from the back. A hive that has lost a significant amount of weight since autumn is consuming stores faster than expected. If hefting indicates a very light hive in January, place a candy board as soon as the outside temperature allows a quick crown-board lift.
